title: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease – MCQ
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: The liver receives what percentage of total cardiac output?
questions.explanation: The liver receives approximately 25% of cardiac output (~1500 mL/min). Of this, 75% comes from the portal vein and 25% from the hepatic artery.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Hepatic Blood Supply

questions.question: What percentage of hepatic blood supply comes from the portal vein?
questions.explanation: The portal vein provides 75% of hepatic blood flow but only 50% of oxygen supply. The hepatic artery provides 25% of blood flow but 50% of oxygen supply.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Portal Vein

questions.question: The hepatic artery buffer response refers to:

options.A: Increase in portal vein flow when hepatic artery flow decreases
options.B: Compensatory increase in hepatic artery flow when portal venous flow decreases
options.C: Decrease in both hepatic artery and portal vein flow during anesthesia
options.D: Shunting of blood through portosystemic collaterals

questions.explanation: The hepatic artery buffer response is a semi-reciprocal mechanism where hepatic arterial flow increases to compensate for reduced portal venous flow, maintaining total hepatic blood flow and oxygen delivery.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Buffer Response

questions.question: Child-Pugh classification assesses liver function based on all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Child-Pugh score uses 5 parameters: Bilirubin, Albumin, Prothrombin time (INR), Ascites, and Encephalopathy grade. Creatinine is part of the MELD score, not Child-Pugh.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Assessment of Liver Function

questions.question: Child-Pugh Class C (score 10–15) is associated with perioperative mortality of approximately:
questions.explanation: Child-Pugh C carries perioperative mortality >50% for major abdominal surgery. Class A: ~10%, Class B: ~30%, Class C: >50%.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Operative Risk

questions.question: The MELD score is calculated from which THREE laboratory values?
questions.explanation: MELD (Model for End-Stage Liver Disease) uses serum Bilirubin, Creatinine, and INR. It is primarily used to prioritize organ allocation for liver transplantation.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: MELD Score

questions.question: In cirrhotic patients, the most reliable liver function test for assessing SYNTHETIC function is:
questions.explanation: PT/INR is the best indicator of hepatic synthetic function because clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X) are synthesized by the liver. Factor VII has the shortest half-life (6 hours), making PT the earliest indicator of synthetic dysfunction.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Coagulation Tests

questions.question: The coagulopathy in liver disease is due to:

options.A: Decreased synthesis of clotting factors only
options.C: Decreased synthesis of clotting factors, thrombocytopenia, hyperfibrinolysis, and dysfibrinogenemia

questions.explanation: Coagulopathy in liver disease is multifactorial: reduced synthesis of ALL clotting factors (except VIII and vWF), thrombocytopenia (splenic sequestration), qualitative platelet defects, hyperfibrinolysis, and dysfibrinogenemia.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Hemostatic Abnormalities

questions.question: Which clotting factor is NOT synthesized by the liver?
questions.explanation: Factor VIII (and von Willebrand factor) is synthesized by vascular endothelium and reticuloendothelial cells, NOT by hepatocytes. All other clotting factors are produced by the liver.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Clotting Factor Synthesis

questions.question: Thrombocytopenia in cirrhosis is primarily caused by:

options.B: Splenic sequestration (hypersplenism) due to portal hypertension

questions.explanation: Portal hypertension causes splenomegaly (congestive), leading to splenic sequestration of up to 90% of circulating platelets. Decreased thrombopoietin production by the diseased liver also contributes.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Platelet Abnormalities

questions.question: Hepatopulmonary syndrome is characterized by:

options.A: Pulmonary hypertension with right heart failure
options.C: Pleural effusion with restrictive lung disease

questions.explanation: Hepatopulmonary syndrome (HPS) consists of the triad: liver disease + intrapulmonary vascular dilatation + arterial hypoxemia (PaO2 <80 mmHg). The dilated pulmonary capillaries cause V/Q mismatch and right-to-left shunting.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Pulmonary Complications

questions.question: Portopulmonary hypertension differs from hepatopulmonary syndrome in that it involves:
questions.explanation: Portopulmonary hypertension (POPH) = portal hypertension + pulmonary arterial hypertension (mean PAP >25 mmHg at rest). Unlike HPS, it involves vasoconstriction and remodeling of pulmonary vessels.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: POPH

questions.question: Hepatorenal syndrome (HRS) is characterized by all EXCEPT:

options.A: Renal failure in advanced liver disease

questions.explanation: HRS is functional renal failure with structurally normal kidneys. Urine sodium is characteristically very low (<10 mEq/L) due to intense renal vasoconstriction and sodium retention. High urine sodium suggests intrinsic renal disease.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: HRS

questions.question: The hemodynamic profile of advanced cirrhosis is characterized by:

options.B: Low SVR, high cardiac output (hyperdynamic circulation)

questions.explanation: Advanced cirrhosis causes a hyperdynamic circulation: increased cardiac output, decreased SVR, decreased MAP, and increased plasma volume. This is due to splanchnic vasodilation mediated by nitric oxide.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Hemodynamic Changes

questions.question: The primary mediator of splanchnic vasodilation in cirrhosis is:
questions.explanation: Nitric oxide (NO) is the primary mediator of splanchnic and systemic vasodilation in cirrhosis. Increased NO production in the splanchnic circulation leads to portal hypertension and the hyperdynamic state.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Vasodilation in Cirrhosis

questions.question: Cirrhotic cardiomyopathy is characterized by all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Cirrhotic cardiomyopathy features impaired systolic response to stress (blunted β-receptor response), diastolic dysfunction, QT prolongation, and DECREASED sensitivity to catecholamines (downregulation of β-receptors).
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Cardiac Complications

questions.question: The most common cause of acute liver failure worldwide is:
questions.explanation: Acetaminophen overdose is the most common cause of acute liver failure in developed countries. In developing countries, viral hepatitis (especially Hepatitis B and E) is more common.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Causes of ALF

questions.question: The antidote for acetaminophen (paracetamol) overdose is:
questions.explanation: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is the specific antidote for paracetamol toxicity. It replenishes hepatic glutathione stores and is most effective within 8 hours of ingestion, but has benefit even up to 72 hours.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Paracetamol Overdose

questions.question: Hepatic encephalopathy is primarily caused by accumulation of:
questions.explanation: Ammonia is the primary neurotoxin in hepatic encephalopathy. The failing liver cannot convert ammonia to urea (urea cycle). Ammonia crosses the blood-brain barrier and causes astrocyte swelling and cerebral edema.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Encephalopathy

questions.question: The drug of choice for treatment of hepatic encephalopathy is:
questions.explanation: Lactulose is the first-line treatment. It acidifies colonic pH, converting ammonia (NH3) to ammonium (NH4+) which cannot be absorbed. It also acts as an osmotic laxative, promoting fecal excretion of ammonia.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Encephalopathy Management

questions.question: In liver disease, the dose of which muscle relaxant needs NO adjustment?
questions.explanation: Atracurium and cisatracurium undergo Hofmann degradation (spontaneous pH and temperature-dependent breakdown) and ester hydrolysis — both independent of hepatic or renal function. They are the neuromuscular blockers of choice in liver disease.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Neuromuscular Blockers

questions.question: The duration of action of succinylcholine may be PROLONGED in liver disease because:
questions.explanation: Plasma cholinesterase (butyrylcholinesterase) is synthesized by the liver. In severe liver disease, reduced synthesis leads to decreased enzyme levels, prolonging the duration of action of succinylcholine and mivacurium.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Cholinesterase

questions.question: In patients with liver cirrhosis, the sensitivity to sedative drugs is INCREASED due to:

options.B: Decreased plasma protein binding, increased free drug, and increased cerebral sensitivity

questions.explanation: Low albumin means more free (unbound) drug, decreased hepatic clearance prolongs drug action, and the encephalopathic brain has increased sensitivity to CNS depressants. All contribute to exaggerated drug effects.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Drug Sensitivity

questions.question: Which opioid is safest to use in liver disease due to its organ-independent metabolism?
questions.explanation: Remifentanil is metabolized by non-specific plasma and tissue esterases, independent of hepatic and renal function. Its pharmacokinetics are essentially unchanged in liver disease.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Analgesics

questions.question: Which volatile anesthetic should be AVOIDED in liver disease due to its potential for hepatotoxicity?
questions.explanation: Halothane can cause two types of hepatotoxicity: Type 1 (mild, self-limiting, 20% incidence) and Type 2 (immune-mediated fulminant hepatic necrosis — 'halothane hepatitis', 1 in 35,000). It is avoided in liver disease patients.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Halothane Hepatitis

questions.question: Halothane hepatitis (Type 2) is mediated by:
questions.explanation: Type 2 halothane hepatitis is immune-mediated. The oxidative metabolite trifluoroacetyl chloride (TFA) binds to liver proteins forming a hapten, triggering an antibody-mediated immune response causing massive hepatic necrosis.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Mechanism of Halothane Hepatotoxicity

questions.question: Among modern volatile agents, which reduces hepatic blood flow the LEAST?
questions.explanation: Isoflurane maintains hepatic arterial blood flow better than other volatile agents due to preservation of the hepatic artery buffer response. Halothane significantly reduces hepatic blood flow.
questions.topic: Volatile Agents and Hepatic Blood Flow
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Effects on Hepatic Perfusion

questions.question: Ascites in cirrhosis is primarily due to:
questions.explanation: Ascites formation requires: portal hypertension (increased hydrostatic pressure), hypoalbuminemia (decreased oncotic pressure), and renal sodium/water retention (due to RAAS activation from splanchnic vasodilation).
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Pathogenesis of Ascites

questions.question: What is the anesthetic significance of large-volume ascites?

options.C: Increased risk of aspiration, reduced FRC, splinting of diaphragm

questions.explanation: Large ascites causes diaphragmatic splinting, reduced FRC, rapid desaturation, increased aspiration risk (full stomach consideration), and may reduce venous return by IVC compression. Rapid sequence intubation may be indicated.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Ascites and Anesthesia

questions.question: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) in a cirrhotic patient with ascites is diagnosed by ascitic fluid neutrophil count of:
questions.explanation: SBP is diagnosed when ascitic fluid absolute neutrophil count exceeds 250 cells/mm3 (with or without positive culture). Empiric antibiotic therapy (cefotaxime) should be started immediately.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: SBP

questions.question: Regional anesthesia (spinal/epidural) in liver disease patients is relatively contraindicated primarily because of:

options.B: Coagulopathy and thrombocytopenia (risk of epidural hematoma)

questions.explanation: The main concern is coagulopathy (elevated INR) and thrombocytopenia, which increase the risk of epidural/spinal hematoma. Neuraxial block is generally avoided if INR >1.5 or platelets <80,000.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Neuraxial Block

questions.question: Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) is given in liver disease to correct coagulopathy because it contains:

options.C: Only Factor VII and Factor IX

questions.explanation: FFP contains all coagulation factors at near-physiological concentrations, including fibrinogen. It is the mainstay for correcting coagulopathy in liver disease, particularly before invasive procedures.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: FFP

questions.question: Vitamin K administration corrects coagulopathy in:
questions.explanation: Vitamin K corrects coagulopathy in obstructive jaundice because the problem is malabsorption of fat-soluble vitamins (K), not impaired synthesis. In hepatocellular disease, the hepatocytes themselves are damaged and cannot synthesize clotting factors even with adequate Vitamin K.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Vitamin K

questions.question: Portal hypertension is defined as portal venous pressure above:
questions.explanation: Normal portal pressure is 5–10 mmHg. Portal hypertension is defined as hepatic venous pressure gradient (HVPG) >5 mmHg or portal pressure >10 mmHg. Clinically significant portal hypertension is HVPG >10 mmHg.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Portal Pressure

questions.question: Esophageal variceal bleeding occurs when portal pressure gradient exceeds:
questions.explanation: Variceal bleeding occurs when HVPG exceeds 12 mmHg. The risk of bleeding increases with variceal size, red color signs on varices, and severity of liver disease.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Esophageal Varices

questions.question: The TIPS (Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt) procedure is used to treat:
questions.explanation: TIPS creates a shunt between the hepatic vein and portal vein, decompressing the portal system. It is used for refractory variceal bleeding and refractory ascites. A major side effect is worsening hepatic encephalopathy.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: TIPS

questions.question: During liver transplantation, the MOST hemodynamically unstable phase is:
questions.explanation: The reperfusion phase (unclamping the new liver) is the most dangerous. Post-reperfusion syndrome occurs in ~30% of patients: severe hypotension (>30% decrease in MAP), bradycardia, hyperkalemia, hypothermia, and possible cardiac arrest.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Reperfusion Phase

questions.question: Post-reperfusion syndrome during liver transplant is defined as:

options.B: >30% decrease in MAP lasting >1 minute within 5 minutes of reperfusion

questions.explanation: Post-reperfusion syndrome = decrease in MAP >30% from baseline, lasting >1 minute, occurring within 5 minutes of portal vein unclamping. It is caused by release of cold, acidotic, hyperkalemic preservation solution and vasoactive mediators from the graft.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Reperfusion Complications

questions.question: The MOST life-threatening electrolyte abnormality during liver transplant reperfusion is:
questions.explanation: Severe hyperkalemia (from preservation solution and ischemic graft washout) during reperfusion can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias. Calcium chloride, insulin/glucose, and sodium bicarbonate should be readily available.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Electrolyte Management

questions.question: Hypoglycemia during the anhepatic phase of liver transplant occurs because:

options.B: Absence of hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis

questions.explanation: During the anhepatic phase, there is no functional liver for gluconeogenesis or glycogenolysis. Blood glucose must be monitored frequently and 5–10% dextrose infusion may be needed.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Metabolic Changes

questions.question: Citrate toxicity from massive blood transfusion in liver transplant causes:
questions.explanation: Citrate (anticoagulant in stored blood) chelates ionized calcium, causing hypocalcemia. The failing/absent liver cannot metabolize citrate. Hypocalcemia leads to myocardial depression, coagulopathy, and hypotension.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Citrate Toxicity

questions.question: The BEST intraoperative monitor for coagulation during liver transplant is:
questions.explanation: TEG/ROTEM provides a comprehensive, rapid, point-of-care assessment of the entire coagulation cascade (including fibrinolysis). It guides targeted blood product therapy during liver transplant.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: TEG in Liver Transplant

questions.question: Obstructive jaundice increases the risk of all EXCEPT:

options.D: Sepsis and wound infection (impaired immune function)

questions.explanation: Obstructive jaundice increases risk of renal failure, coagulopathy (malabsorption of fat-soluble Vitamin K), sepsis/wound infection, and endotoxemia. Neuromuscular blocker sensitivity is not specifically increased.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Surgical Risks in Jaundice

questions.question: Preoperative biliary drainage before surgery for obstructive jaundice is recommended to:

options.B: Reduce the risk of renal failure and sepsis

questions.explanation: Preoperative biliary drainage reduces serum bilirubin, improves coagulation (Vitamin K absorption), and reduces the risk of postoperative renal failure and sepsis, particularly when bilirubin is >10 mg/dL.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Preoperative Preparation

questions.question: To prevent renal failure in patients with obstructive jaundice, the key preoperative measure is:
questions.explanation: Adequate preoperative hydration is crucial. Mannitol infusion (to maintain urine output) before surgery helps prevent renal tubular damage from bile salt nephrotoxicity in obstructive jaundice.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Renal Protection

questions.question: Phase I drug metabolism (oxidation, reduction, hydrolysis) in liver disease is:
questions.explanation: Phase I reactions (cytochrome P450-dependent) are significantly impaired in liver disease due to reduced enzyme activity and hepatic blood flow. Phase II reactions (conjugation) are relatively preserved until advanced disease.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Hepatic Drug Metabolism

questions.question: Which benzodiazepine is preferred in liver disease due to its simpler metabolism?
questions.explanation: Oxazepam and lorazepam undergo only Phase II conjugation (glucuronidation), which is preserved in liver disease. Diazepam and midazolam require Phase I oxidation, which is impaired in liver disease.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Sedatives

questions.question: The most common cause of postoperative jaundice is:

options.B: Increased bilirubin load (blood transfusion, hematoma reabsorption)

questions.explanation: The most common cause of postoperative jaundice is increased bilirubin production from blood transfusions (hemolysis of stored blood), hematoma reabsorption, and hemolytic reactions, not hepatocellular damage.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Causes of Postop Jaundice

questions.question: In a patient with liver cirrhosis undergoing emergency surgery, the MOST important preoperative correction is:
questions.explanation: Correction of coagulopathy is the highest priority before surgery to prevent catastrophic intraoperative bleeding. Target INR <1.5 (with FFP), platelets >50,000 (with platelet transfusion), and fibrinogen >100 mg/dL.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Emergency Surgery in Cirrhosis

questions.question: Which monitoring is essential during major surgery in a cirrhotic patient?

options.B: Invasive arterial BP, CVP, urine output, temperature, and serial ABGs/electrolytes
options.C: Only non-invasive BP and pulse oximetry

questions.explanation: Major surgery in cirrhotic patients requires invasive arterial monitoring (beat-to-beat BP and ABG sampling), CVP (fluid management), urinary catheter (renal function), temperature monitoring, and serial labs (glucose, electrolytes, coagulation).
questions.reference: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease, Section: Intraoperative Monitoring
